request.question: What company published a fantasy tabletop role-playing game originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson that has a book of rules that lack the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game?

title: Player's Handbook
Blackmoor (supplement)
Dungeons & Dragons (1974)
Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set
Dungeons & Dragons
Greyhawk (supplement)
Paranoia (role-playing game)
Gary Gygax
Don't Give Up the Ship!
Greyhawk
sentences: The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").
It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.
Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".
Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.

Blackmoor is a supplementary rulebook (product designation TSR 2004) of the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game written by Dave Arneson (with a foreword by Gary Gygax).
The original Dungeons & Dragons (commonly abbreviated "D&D") boxed set by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson was published by TSR, Inc. in 1974.
It initially included the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
Its product designation was TSR 2002.
The Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set is a set of rulebooks for the "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") fantasy role-playing game.
First published in 1977, it saw a handful of revisions and reprintings.
The first edition was written by J. Eric Holmes based on Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson's original work.
Later editions were edited by Tom Moldvay, Dave Cook, and Frank Mentzer.

Dungeons & Dragons (abbreviated as D&D or DnD) is a fantasy tabletop role-playing game (RPG) originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, and first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR).
The game has been published by Wizards of the Coast (now a subsidiary of Hasbro) since 1997.
It was derived from miniature wargames with a variation of the "Chainmail" game serving as the initial rule system.
"D&D"'s publication is commonly recognized as the beginning of modern role-playing games and the role-playing game industry.
Greyhawk is a supplementary rulebook written by Gary Gygax and Robert J. Kuntz for the original edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
It has been called "the first and most important supplement" to the original "D&D" rules.
By adding a combat system, it severed all ties with "Chainmail", making "D&D" a truly stand-alone game system.

Although the name of the book was taken from the home campaign supervised by Gygax and Kuntz based on Gygax's imagined Castle Greyhawk and the lands surrounding it, "Greyhawk" did not give any details of the castle or the campaign world; instead, it explained the rules that Gygax and Kuntz used in their home campaign, and introduced a number of character classes, spells, concepts and monsters used in all subsequent editions of "D&D".
Paranoia is a dystopian science-fiction tabletop role-playing game originally designed and written by Greg Costikyan, Dan Gelber, and Eric Goldberg, and first published in 1984 by West End Games.
Since 2004 the game has been published under license by Mongoose Publishing.
The game won the Origins Award for Best Roleplaying Rules of 1984 and was inducted into the Origins Awards Hall of Fame in 2007.

"Paranoia" is notable among tabletop games for being more competitive than co-operative, with players encouraged to betray one another for their own interests, as well as for keeping a light-hearted, tongue in cheek tone despite its dystopian setting.

Ernest Gary Gygax ( ) (July 27, 1938 – March 4, 2008) was an American game designer and author best known for co-creating the pioneering role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D") with Dave Arneson.
Gygax has been described as the father of "D&D".
Don't Give Up the Ship is a set of rules for conducting Napoleonic era naval wargames.
The game was published by Guidon Games in 1972 and republished by TSR, Inc. in 1975.
It was the first collaboration between Dave Arneson and Gary Gygax, the co-creators of "Dungeons & Dragons".
"D&D" fans may also recognize the name of contributor Mike Carr, who edited the rules and researched the historical single ship actions that are included as game scenarios.
Greyhawk, also known as the World of Greyhawk, is a fictional world designed as a campaign setting for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy roleplaying game.

Although not the first campaign world developed for "Dungeons & Dragons"—Dave Arneson's "Blackmoor" campaign predated it by over a year — the world of Greyhawk was the setting most closely identified with the development of the game from 1972 until 2008.

The world itself started as a simple dungeon under a castle designed by Gary Gygax for the amusement of his children and friends, but it rapidly expanded to include not only a complex multi-layered dungeon environment, but also the nearby city of Greyhawk, and eventually an entire world.

In addition to the campaign world, which was published in several editions over twenty years, Greyhawk was also used as the setting for many adventures published in support of the game, as well as for RPGA's massively shared "Living Greyhawk" campaign from 2000–2008.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Dungeons & Dragons; Player's Handbook; publishers (Tactical Studies Rules / TSR; Wizards of the Coast).

- Dungeons & Dragons — "originally designed by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson" and "first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR)." (passage 1)
- Dungeons & Dragons — "has been published by Wizards of the Coast ... since 1997." (passage 1)
- Player's Handbook — "does not contain the complete set of rules for the game" and "only includes rules for use by players of the game." (passage 3)
- Ambiguity / missing fact: which publisher is intended by the question — the "first published" company (TSR) or the current publisher (Wizards of the Coast)? Also missing: which edition/printing of the Player's Handbook and its specific publisher. Bridge entity for next hop: "Dungeons & Dragons" and the "Player's Handbook" link publishers to the question.
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. Dungeons & Dragons — lede/infobox and "Publication" or "History" section (confirm original publisher and publication timeline).
  2. Tactical Studies Rules (TSR, Inc.) — lede/History or publications list (verify TSR published D&D in 1974).
  3. Player's Handbook (Dungeons & Dragons) — lede/infobox and "Publication history" or "Editions" (confirm which company published the Player's Handbook edition described).
  4. Wizards of the Coast — lede/History (confirm takeover/publishing since 1997).
hop2_docs: Player's Handbook II | Player's Handbook II is the title of a third edition "Dungeons & Dragons" supplement.  It is a handbook of rules and guidelines for the "Dungeons & Dragons" role-playing game.  As the name implies, the book is a supplement to the edition's "Player's Handbook".  It introduces supplemental rules, new spells and new classes.

Dungeons & Dragons Adventure Game | The Dungeons & Dragons Adventure Game is the name of two companion accessories to the second and third editions of the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.  Designed as simpler, stand-alone versions of "Dungeons & Dragons", they feature a simplified ruleset, but with character progression that parallels the standard game.  However, for 3rd-level characters and higher, the standard "Player's Handbook" is still required.  The first version was published in 1999 for the second edition of "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons", while the second version was published in 2000 for the third edition of "Dungeons & Dragons".

Player's Handbook | The Player's Handbook (Players Handbook in first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" ("AD&D")) is a book of rules for the fantasy role-playing game "Dungeons & Dragons" ("D&D").  It does not contain the complete set of rules for the game, and only includes rules for use by players of the game.  Additional rules, for use by Dungeon Masters (DMs), who referee the game, can be found in the "Dungeon Master's Guide".  Many optional rules, such as those governing extremely high-level players, and some of the more obscure spells, are found in other sources.
Player's Handbook 2 | Player's Handbook 2 is a supplement to the 4th edition of the "Dungeons & Dragons" role-playing game.

Arcana Unearthed | Arcana Unearthed (properly Monte Cook's Arcana Unearthed, ISBN  ) is a role-playing game created by Monte Cook and first published in 2003.  Described as a "variant player's handbook", the 256-page hardcover core rulebook bears many similarities to the "Players Handbook" of 3rd Edition "Dungeons & Dragons", on which Cook worked a few years prior.  In terms of rules, "Arcana Unearthed" is based on the d20 system of "Dungeons & Dragons", although due to including rules for character advancement it cannot be an official d20 system product.  (It does, however, use the Open Gaming License.)

The Official RPGA Tournament Handbook | The Official RPGA Handbook is an adventure module for the "Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game, set in the game's World of Greyhawk campaign setting.  TSR, Inc. published the module in 1987 for the first edition "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" rules.  It is part of the "C" series of modules, a set of unrelated adventures originally designed for competition play ("c" being the first letter in "competition").
The Complete Fighter's Handbook | The Complete Fighter's Handbook is a supplemental rulebook published in 1989 for the 2nd edition of the "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game.
response.summary_2: Dungeons & Dragons was first published in 1974 by Tactical Studies Rules, Inc. (TSR). The Player's Handbook does not contain the complete set of rules and includes only rules for use by players, with additional referee rules found in the Dungeon Master's Guide.
